professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Many universities have begun offering courses entirely in second languages — not just language classes, but content courses like physics or economics taught in English to non-native speakers. Schools in Sweden, the Netherlands, and South Korea have adopted this model widely, arguing it prepares students for global careers and attracts international talent. Critics worry that learning complex concepts in a second language reduces depth of understanding and pushes the native language out of academic life. Before our next class, I'd like you to consider: should universities expand content courses taught in a second language, or keep advanced instruction in the country's primary language?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I think expanding these programs is the right move. My cousin studies engineering in the Netherlands and all her core lectures are in English, and she says it's opened doors she wouldn't have had otherwise — internships at multinational firms, research collaborations across Europe. The reality is that academic papers in fields like computer science or biomedicine are written in English. If students never engage with serious material in that language, they hit a wall when they try to enter international research or industry. Yes, the first semester is harder, but the long-term payoff seems clear.

students.name: Mark
students.text: Honestly, I worry about what gets lost. When students learn quantum mechanics or microeconomics in a language they're still mastering, the discussion in seminars often becomes shallower — people stick to safe vocabulary instead of pushing back on ideas. There's also a cultural cost. If a country's universities stop teaching advanced subjects in the local language, that language gradually loses the technical vocabulary to discuss those fields. I'd rather see strong second-language electives alongside rigorous instruction in the native language, not a wholesale shift.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Several cities — Paris, Bogotá, and parts of Tokyo — have closed major streets to private cars on weekends, opening them to pedestrians, cyclists, and street vendors. Paris has even made some closures permanent, transforming arterial roads along the Seine into car-free promenades. Supporters say these spaces revive neighborhood life and cut pollution; opponents argue they push traffic onto residential side streets and hurt small businesses that depend on car-borne customers. For our discussion this week: should mid-sized cities follow Paris's example and convert major streets into permanent car-free zones, or is this a luxury that only dense, transit-rich cities can afford?

students.name: Helen
students.text: I'd push for more car-free streets, even in mid-sized cities. When I visited Pontevedra in Spain — a small city of about 80,000 people — almost the entire historic center was pedestrianized, and the place was buzzing with families and outdoor cafes. The shop owners I spoke with said foot traffic actually went up after the change, not down. The fear that closing streets kills businesses comes mostly from drivers who assume their habits represent everyone's. Cities that commit to this transition end up with denser, more walkable retail, not empty storefronts.

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I see Helen's point about Pontevedra, but I think the comparison breaks down for many American mid-sized cities. Places like Tulsa or Dayton were literally designed around driving — the housing is spread out, transit barely exists, and people often drive twenty minutes just to reach a grocery store. Closing a major street there without first building real bus and bike infrastructure would just shove cars onto already-congested side roads. I'd support gradual, weekend-only closures while transit improves, but a permanent shift requires years of groundwork that most mid-sized cities haven't done.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about algorithmic hiring tools. A growing number of large employers — Unilever, IBM, Hilton — now use AI systems to screen job applicants by analyzing résumés, video interviews, or even gameplay on simple cognitive tasks. Vendors claim these tools reduce human bias and process candidates faster than overworked recruiters can. Critics counter that the algorithms inherit bias from past hiring data and that applicants rarely understand how decisions are made. Should companies be allowed to use AI as the primary filter for hiring, or should a human always make the first cut?

students.name: Cameron
students.text: I lean toward letting companies use these tools, but with strict guardrails. Human recruiters skim hundreds of résumés in minutes and make decisions based on whatever stands out — that's also a flawed process, and it produces well-documented bias against names, gaps in employment, even unfamiliar universities. A well-audited algorithm can be tested for disparate impact in ways a single recruiter never is. The key is regulation: require companies to disclose what the system measures and to publish bias audits annually. Banning the tools entirely just returns us to the noisier human-only version.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I don't think the comparison to flawed humans is enough to justify this. When a recruiter rejects me, I can sometimes get feedback or a referral that helps me improve. When an algorithm rejects me, there's a black box and no recourse. Worse, the bias scales — one biased recruiter affects a few hundred candidates a year, while one biased model can screen out millions. I'd require at least a human reviewer for any final rejection, so accountability stays with a person who can explain and be held responsible for the decision.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed in class, governments and private companies are debating whether to build the next generation of particle colliders — successors to CERN's Large Hadron Collider — at costs estimated above twenty billion dollars. Proponents argue these machines have driven discoveries from the Higgs boson to medical imaging advances, and that staying at the frontier is essential for pure science. Critics, including some prominent physicists, argue that the scientific return per dollar has been falling and that money would be better spent on smaller experiments or applied research with clearer payoffs. Should governments fund the next-generation collider, or has 'big physics' reached the point of diminishing returns?

students.name: Steve
students.text: I think we should keep building these machines. The history of physics shows that fundamental discoveries often look pointless at the moment they happen and then transform technology decades later — Maxwell's equations gave us electrical grids, quantum mechanics gave us semiconductors. Walking away from the energy frontier means choosing not to look for the next surprise. Twenty billion dollars sounds enormous, but it's roughly what the U.S. spends on subsidizing corn ethanol in a single year. Framing big physics as wasteful while we burn money on far less productive things seems backwards to me.

students.name: Diane
students.text: I'm honestly more skeptical here. The Higgs discovery was over a decade ago, and the new colliders are being pitched without a clear target — supporters often just say 'we might find something.' That's not how I'd want to spend twenty billion in public money. Smaller experiments in dark matter detection, neutrino physics, and quantum simulation are producing results and training many more physicists per dollar. Cutting one mega-project would let us fund dozens of high-payoff alternatives. Sometimes saying no to the biggest possible machine is what mature science looks like.
